title: Analysis: Trump is normalizing things that would have been scandals in his first term | CNN Politics
text:
Source: CNN

One of the earliest scandals ofDonald Trump’sfirst term came when the FBI director he had just fired, James Comey, testified that the president had demanded his loyalty.

This was a problem for two reasons: because FBI directors are supposed be independent and because Comey happened to be investigating Trump.

The president, the White House and Trump’s legal team all denied Comey’s claim.

Fast forward nine years, and Trump is once again looking for loyalty in all the wrong places — except this time very publicly. Hecasually suggestedover the weekend that Supreme Court justices he appointed — public servants who, like an FBI director, are not supposed to show favoritism — should be more loyal to him than they have been.

But today, Trump’s social media missive barely feels like news.

The juxtaposition epitomizes how Trump is doing things in his second term that would have been major controversies in his first but barely register now.

That’s because he’s spent a decade gradually upping the provocations and wearing down his critics’ willingness to raise a fuss.

Trump claimed in his Sundaysocial media postthat Democratic-appointed justices were more politically loyal to presidents who nominated them, while complaining that he has to deal with the likes of Neil Gorsuch and Amy Coney Barrett ruling against him on tariffs.

“They have to do the right thing,” Trump said of the justices, “but it’s really OK for them to be loyal to the person that appointed them to ‘almost’ the highest position in the land, that is, a Justice of the United States Supreme Court.”

Trump claimed, “I don’t want loyalty, but I do want and expect it for our Country.” But the rest of the message made abundantly clear he was asking for loyalty to himself and his agenda.

(Trump, for instance, didn’t focus on George W. Bush-nominated Chief Justice John Roberts, who also sided against him on tariffs. And he mentioned no fewer than three times that he had appointed Gorsuch and Barrett.)

This kind of public pressure would have been a very big deal nine years ago.

For example, when Comey testified in June 2017 that Trump had told him, even amid the early parts of the Russia investigation, “I need loyalty, I expect loyalty,” the president and many around him denied it.

“I hardly know the man. I’m not going to say, ‘I want you to pledge allegiance,’” Trump said at the time, adding: “It doesn’t make sense. No, I didn’t say that.”

And Trump lawyer Marc Kasowitz made clear the president’s team wasn’t just playing word games by quibbling with how he was quoted.

“The president never told Mr. Comey, ‘I need loyalty, I expect loyalty’ in form or substance,” Kasowitz said.

But in 2026, a similar episode barely registers.

The situations aren’t completely analogous. The Supreme Court isn’t investigating Trump, as Comey’s FBI was. But it has heard cases involving key administration policies and even Trump’s personal criminal liability, and it will likely continue to play a huge role given the president’s penchant for pushing the envelope.

Indeed, asking for loyalty from one-third of the Supreme Court — Trump appointed three of the nine justices — might in some ways be a bigger deal than asking for it from the FBI director. An FBI director can’t convict a president on their own, but justices get to decide many huge cases.

But his pressure on the court doesn’t register like the alleged Comey request because Trump has spent a decade boiling the frog.

Through a steadily escalating series of provocations, Trump turns once unthinkable actions into things that seem more commonplace. He wears down those who would object — be they Democrats, other critics or even some fellow Republicans — and makes it seem like old news.

He’s done it with politically oriented investigations of his foes; his attorneys general once said they wouldn’t abide by any Trump requests to investigate foes, but today acting Attorney General Todd Blanchetreats it as no big deal.

Trump’s done it with self-enrichment while serving in the Oval Office, going from the “emoluments” controversy in his first term toopenly flouting ethics rulesin his second term.

He did it with mass firings of inspectors general, whom Republicans like Senate Judiciary Committee Chair Chuck Grassleystrove to protect in Trump’s first term.

He’s done it with a ramped-up series of politically oriented pardons that would have spurred congressional investigations in the past.

And he’s done it withincreasingly open xenophobia— even publicly making comments that sound a lot like things he denied saying in the late 2010s (i.e. “shithole countries”).

As with that, Trump has been fertilizing the my-justices-should-be-loyal tree for a long time. And he’s increasingly voicing it out in the open.

That doesn’t mean the conservative justices he appointedwillbe loyal; they’ve proved they’ll buck him on tariffs and could soon do so on birthright citizenship — the main subject of Trump’s social media post. Indeed, it’s possible his requestcould backfireif the justices feel compelled to protect the perception of their independence.

But Trump is normalizing asking for loyalty from public servants whom our system of government designed to be above such things.

treats it as no big deal
strove to protect in Trump’s first term
